title: Antiphon

infobox.value: ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") ἀντίφωνον, ἀντί "opposite" and φωνή "voice") is a short [chant](/wiki/Chant "Chant") in [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") [ritual](/wiki/Ritual "Ritual"), sung as a [refrain](/wiki/Refrain "Refrain"). The texts of antiphons are usually taken from the [Psalms](/wiki/Psalms "Psalms") or Scripture, but may also be freely composed. Their form was favored by [St. Ambrose](/wiki/St._Ambrose "St. Ambrose") and they feature prominently in [Ambrosian chant](/wiki/Ambrosian_chant "Ambrosian chant"), but they are used widely in [Gregorian chant](/wiki/Gregorian_chant "Gregorian chant") as well. They may be used during Mass, for the [Introit](/wiki/Introit "Introit"), the [Offertory](/wiki/Offertory "Offertory") or the [Communion](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy)#Communion_rite "Mass (liturgy)"). They may also be used in the [Liturgy of the Hours](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Hours "Liturgy of the Hours"), typically for [Lauds](/wiki/Lauds "Lauds") or [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers").

is generally used for any [call and response](/wiki/Call_and_response_(music) "Call and response (music)") style of singing, such as the [kirtan](/wiki/Kirtan "Kirtan") or the [sea shanty](/wiki/Sea_shanty "Sea shanty") and other work songs, and songs and worship in African and African-American culture. **Antiphonal music** is that performed by two choirs in interaction, often singing alternate musical phrases.[[1]](#cite_note-1) *Antiphonal psalmody* is the [singing](/wiki/Singing "Singing") or musical playing of [psalms](/wiki/Psalm "Psalm") by alternating groups of performers.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The term "antiphony" can also refer to a choir-book containing antiphons.
E. Foley and M. Paul, *Worship music: a concise dictionary* (Liturgical Press, 2000), p. 18.
J. McKinnon, *Music in early Christian literature* ([Cambridge University Press](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Press "Cambridge University Press"), 1989), p. 10.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Short chant in Christian ritual

This article is about the musical term. For other uses, see [Antiphon (disambiguation)](/wiki/Antiphon_(disambiguation) "Antiphon (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Liber_Responsorialis_1895_p054.jpg/250px-Liber_Responsorialis_1895_p054.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liber_Responsorialis_1895_p054.jpg)

The *Liber responsorialis*, showing on the right-hand page the antiphons for the first [night office](/wiki/Matins "Matins") of Christmas. The associated [psalm tones](/wiki/Psalm_tone "Psalm tone") are indicated by number and ending pitch, and the pitches for the ending of the [doxology](/wiki/Doxology "Doxology") are indicated by the mnemonic *[Euouae](/wiki/Euouae "Euouae")*.

An **antiphon** ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") ἀντίφωνον, ἀντί "opposite" and φωνή "voice") is a short [chant](/wiki/Chant "Chant") in [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") [ritual](/wiki/Ritual "Ritual"), sung as a [refrain](/wiki/Refrain "Refrain"). The texts of antiphons are usually taken from the [Psalms](/wiki/Psalms "Psalms") or Scripture, but may also be freely composed. Their form was favored by [St. Ambrose](/wiki/St._Ambrose "St. Ambrose") and they feature prominently in [Ambrosian chant](/wiki/Ambrosian_chant "Ambrosian chant"), but they are used widely in [Gregorian chant](/wiki/Gregorian_chant "Gregorian chant") as well. They may be used during Mass, for the [Introit](/wiki/Introit "Introit"), the [Offertory](/wiki/Offertory "Offertory") or the [Communion](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy)#Communion_rite "Mass (liturgy)"). They may also be used in the [Liturgy of the Hours](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Hours "Liturgy of the Hours"), typically for [Lauds](/wiki/Lauds "Lauds") or [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers").

They should not be confused with [Marian antiphons](/wiki/Hymns_to_Mary#Marian_antiphons "Hymns to Mary") or [processional antiphons](/w/index.php?title=Processional_antiphon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Processional antiphon (page does not exist)").

When a chant consists of alternating [verses](/wiki/Verse_(music) "Verse (music)") (usually sung by a cantor) and [responses](/wiki/Response_(liturgy) "Response (liturgy)") (usually sung by the congregation), a refrain is needed.

The looser term **antiphony** is generally used for any [call and response](/wiki/Call_and_response_(music) "Call and response (music)") style of singing, such as the [kirtan](/wiki/Kirtan "Kirtan") or the [sea shanty](/wiki/Sea_shanty "Sea shanty") and other work songs, and songs and worship in African and African-American culture. **Antiphonal music** is that performed by two choirs in interaction, often singing alternate musical phrases.[[1]](#cite_note-1) *Antiphonal psalmody* is the [singing](/wiki/Singing "Singing") or musical playing of [psalms](/wiki/Psalm "Psalm") by alternating groups of performers.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The term "antiphony" can also refer to a choir-book containing antiphons.

## Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antiphon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origins")]

The chant of early Christianity through to the end of the 5th century had its root in the [synagogue](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue"), whence early Christians borrowed the traditions of the chanting of psalms, singing of [hymns](/wiki/Hymn "Hymn") and [cantillation](/wiki/Hebrew_cantillation "Hebrew cantillation"). There is some evidence from [Acts of the Apostles](/wiki/Acts_of_the_Apostles "Acts of the Apostles") that early Christians stayed close to contemporary Jewish traditions. For example, Acts 2:46–47 states that "with one accord in the Temple, and breaking bread from house to house did eat their meat with gladness and singleness of heart, praising God, and having favour with all the people".[[3]](#cite_note-wellesz-3) [Socrates of Constantinople](/wiki/Socrates_of_Constantinople "Socrates of Constantinople") wrote that antiphony was introduced into [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") worship by [Ignatius of Antioch](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Antioch "Ignatius of Antioch") (died 107) after he saw a vision of two choirs of angels.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Antiphonal singing was an element of [Jewish liturgy](/wiki/Jewish_liturgy "Jewish liturgy") believed to have entered the monasteries of Syria and Palestine in the 4th century from the Jewish communities such as the one in [Antioch](/wiki/Antioch "Antioch").[[3]](#cite_note-wellesz-3)

Antiphons have remained an integral part of the worship in the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") and [Armenian Rite](/wiki/Armenian_Rite "Armenian Rite").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The practice did not become part of the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") until more than two centuries later. [Ambrose](/wiki/Ambrose "Ambrose") and [Gregory the Great](/wiki/Gregory_the_Great "Gregory the Great"), who are known for their contributions to the formulation of Gregorian chant, are credited with '[antiphonaries](/wiki/Antiphonary "Antiphonary")', collections of works suitable for antiphon, which are still used in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") today.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Polyphonic votive antiphons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antiphon&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Polyphonic votive antiphons")]

[Polyphonic](/wiki/Polyphony "Polyphony") [Marian antiphons](/wiki/Hymns_to_Mary#Marian_antiphons "Hymns to Mary") in the [Tudor votive style](/wiki/Tudor_votive_style "Tudor votive style") emerged in England in the 14th century as settings of texts honouring the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary"), which were sung separately from the [mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") and [office](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Hours "Liturgy of the Hours"), often after [Compline](/wiki/Compline "Compline").[[7]](#cite_note-W._Baxter_Robison_2002_p._363-7) Towards the end of the 15th century, [English](/wiki/English_people "English people") [composers](/wiki/Composer "Composer") produced expanded settings up to nine [parts](/wiki/Part_(music) "Part (music)"), with increasing complexity and [vocal range](/wiki/Vocal_range "Vocal range").[[7]](#cite_note-W._Baxter_Robison_2002_p._363-7) The largest collection of such antiphons is the late-15th-century *[Eton Choirbook](/wiki/Eton_Choirbook "Eton Choirbook")*.[[8]](#cite_note-8) As a result, antiphony remains particularly common in the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") musical tradition: the singers often face each other, placed in the [quire's](/wiki/Choir_(architecture) "Choir (architecture)") *[Decani](/wiki/Decani "Decani")* and *[Cantoris](/wiki/Cantoris "Cantoris")*.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Greater Advent antiphons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antiphon&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Greater Advent antiphons")]

Main article: [O Antiphons](/wiki/O_Antiphons "O Antiphons")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Meister_der_Braunschweig-Magdeburger_Schule_001.jpg/120px-Meister_der_Braunschweig-Magdeburger_Schule_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meister_der_Braunschweig-Magdeburger_Schule_001.jpg)

The [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation")

The Greater Advent or O Antiphons are antiphons used at daily [prayer](/wiki/Prayer "Prayer") in the evenings of the last days of [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") in various [liturgical](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy") [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") traditions.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Each antiphon is a name of [Christ](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus"), one of his attributes mentioned in Scripture. In the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") tradition, they are sung as antiphons to the [Magnificat](/wiki/Magnificat "Magnificat") in [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") from December 17 to December 23.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") they have traditionally been used as antiphons to the Magnificat at [Evening Prayer](/wiki/Evening_Prayer_(Anglican) "Evening Prayer (Anglican)").[[12]](#cite_note-12) More recently they have found a place in primary liturgical documents throughout the Anglican Communion, including the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England")'s *[Common Worship](/wiki/Common_Worship "Common Worship")* [liturgy](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy"). Use of the O Antiphons was preserved in [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") at the [German Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), and they continue to be sung in Lutheran churches.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Polychoral antiphony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antiphon&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Polychoral antiphony")]

When two or more groups of singers sing in alternation, the style of music can also be called *polychoral*. Specifically, this term is usually applied to music of the late [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_music "Renaissance music") and early [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music") periods. Polychoral techniques are a definitive characteristic of the music of the [Venetian school](/wiki/Venetian_School_(music) "Venetian School (music)"), exemplified by the works of [Giovanni Gabrieli](/wiki/Giovanni_Gabrieli "Giovanni Gabrieli"): this music is often known as the [Venetian polychoral style](/wiki/Venetian_polychoral_style "Venetian polychoral style").[[14]](#cite_note-14) The Venetian polychoral style was an important innovation of the late [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_music "Renaissance music"). This style, with its variations as it spread across Europe after 1600, helped to define the beginning of the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music") era. Polychoral music was not limited to [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") in the Renaissance; it was also popular in France with [Marc-Antoine Charpentier](/wiki/Marc-Antoine_Charpentier "Marc-Antoine Charpentier") (37 settings H.16–H.52), in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). There are examples from the 19th and 20th centuries, from composers as diverse as [Hector Berlioz](/wiki/Hector_Berlioz "Hector Berlioz"), [Igor Stravinsky](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky"), [Béla Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók"), and [Karlheinz Stockhausen](/wiki/Karlheinz_Stockhausen "Karlheinz Stockhausen").[[15]](#cite_note-15)
